text:
a) Blood plasma is the liquid component of blood that makes up approximately 55% of its total content. It is a clear, straw-colored fluid that serves as the medium in which the other blood components, such as red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets, are suspended. Plasma is mostly composed of water (about 92%), with the remaining 8% consisting of various substances including proteins (like albumin, globulins, and fibrinogen), nutrients, hormones, gases, waste products, and ions.

b) The role of blood plasma is multifaceted. It acts as a transport medium, carrying nutrients, hormones, and gases to cells and organs throughout the body, and waste products from cells to organs like the liver and kidneys for excretion. Plasma also plays a critical role in maintaining blood pressure and helping to regulate body temperature. Additionally, it contains clotting factors that are essential for blood coagulation, preventing excessive bleeding when a blood vessel is injured.

c) The ABO blood group system has four main blood groups, which are A, B, AB, and O. These groups are determined by the presence or absence of specific antigens on the surface of red blood cells. Group A has A antigens, group B has B antigens, group AB has both A and B antigens, and group O has neither A nor B antigens.

d) The factor that determines whether a blood group is positive (+) or negative (—) is the presence or absence of the Rh factor (also known as the Rhesus factor). If an individual's blood contains the Rh factor, their blood group is classified as Rh-positive, and if it does not, their blood group is classified as Rh-negative.

e) It is crucial to know a person's blood group because transfusing blood of an incompatible group can lead to a severe reaction, known as a hemolytic transfusion reaction. This reaction occurs when the immune system of the recipient identifies the transfused blood cells as foreign and attacks them, which can be life-threatening. Knowing a person's blood group ensures that they receive compatible blood in case of a transfusion, thereby preventing such adverse reactions.

f) The primary function of platelets in the blood is to facilitate blood clotting. When a blood vessel is injured, platelets are activated and aggregate at the site of injury, forming a platelet plug that temporarily seals the wound. This plug serves as a foundation for the subsequent formation of a blood clot, which is stabilized by fibrin, a protein produced through the coagulation cascade. Platelets also release chemical signals that attract more platelets and other cells to the site, promoting further clot formation and helping to stop bleeding.
